Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## SEQUENTIAL COVARIATE SHIFT DETECTION USING CLASSIFIER TWO-SAMPLE TESTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

A standard assumption in supervised learning is that the training data and test data are from the same distribution. However, this assumption often fails to hold in practice, which can cause the learned model to perform poorly. We consider the problem of detecting _covariate shift_ , where the covariate distribution shifts but the conditional distribution of labels given covariates remains the same. This problem can naturally be solved using a two-sample test— _i.e._ , test whether the current test distribution of covariates equals the training distribution of covariates. Our algorithm builds on _classifier tests_ , which train a discriminator to distinguish train and test covariates, and then use the accuracy of this discriminator as a test statistic. A key challenge is that classifier tests assume given a fixed set of test covariates. In practice, test covariates often arrive sequentially over time—e.g., a self-driving car observes a stream of images while driving. Furthermore, covariate shift can occur multiple times— _i.e._ , shift and then shift back later or gradually shift over time. To address these challenges, our algorithm trains the discriminator online. Furthermore, it evaluates test accuracy using each new covariate before taking a gradient step; this strategy avoids constructing a held-out test set, which can reduce sample efficiency. We prove that this optimization preserves the correctness— _i.e._ , our algorithm achieves a desired bound on the false positive rate. In our experiments, we show that our algorithm efficiently detects covariate shifts on ImageNet.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A key challenge facing deep neural networks is their sensitivity to changes in the data distribution. In particular, supervised learning traditionally assumes that the training and test data are from the same distribution (Vapnik, 1998), but this assumption often fails in practice. For example, an autonomous car using perception to identify obstacles needs to be robust to shifts such as changes in the weather and lighting conditions. We focus on _covariate shift_ (Shimodaira, 2000), where there is a shift in the covariate distribution _p_ ( _x_ ), and the conditional label distribution _p_ ( _y | x_ ) remains unchanged. Covariate shift can reduce model performance (Sugiyama & M¨uller, 2005), invalidate uncertainty estimates (Ovadia et al., 2019; Park et al., 2020), and affect model selection (Sugiyama et al., 2007).

One strategy is to devise an algorithm to detect covariate shift; if detected, the algorithm can alert the user that predictions may be unreliable. Covariate shift detection can be formulated as two-sample hypothesis test (Gretton et al., 2012a; Rabanser et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020), where the goal is to determine whether two sets of examples are from the same distribution. To test for covariate shift, we choose the first sample to be the data used to train the model and the second sample to be recent test data given as input to the model. Then, the detector returns “covariate shift” if the hypothesis test indicates that the two samples are from different distributions and “no shift” otherwise.

We propose a detection algorithm based on classifier tests (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Cheng & Cloninger, 2019; Kim et al., 2021), which use the accuracy of a classifier trained to distinguish the two samples as the test statistic. In particular, if the two samples are from the same distribution, then the accuracy should be 1 _/_ 2; otherwise, it should be _>_ 1 _/_ 2. Since the test statistic follows a binomial distribution, we use the Clopper-Pearson interval (Clopper & Pearson, 1934) (an exact confidence interval for the unknown success probability of the Binomial distribution) to derive the cutoff. In contrast, prior work relies on asymptotics to derive the cutoff, which results in approximations.

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

A key challenge is that the test examples are typically obtained over time—e.g., an autonomous robot continuously perceives its environment, and we want to detect if its distribution of observations shifts at any time. There are two key challenges to leveraging classifier tests in this setting. First, they rely on training a classifier to distinguish training and test examples; doing so on every step would be computationally intractable. Second, they rely on a held-out test set to estimate the test statistic, but constructing such a set online would reduce sample efficiency.

Rather than train a classifier at each step, our proposed algorithm trains a model online using stochastic gradient descent. Then, rather than construct a held-out test set, our algorithm evaluates the accuracy of the model online using each example before taking a gradient step on that example. We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold. In addition, we prove bounds on the false negative rate under mild conditions on the classifier (i.e., it achieves nontrivial accuracy distinguishing the two distributions).

We evaluate our approach on both synthetic and natural shifts on the ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015) dataset. In particular, we demonstrate that our approach achieves better sample efficiency than baseline algorithms; furthermore, it uniformly satisfies the desired false positive rate. Thus, our algorithm is an effective strategy for sequential covariate shift detection.

**Contributions.** We formulate (sequential) covariate shift detection as a two-sample test, and propose a novel algorithm to solve this problem (Section 3). Then, we prove finite sample bounds on false positive rate and false negative rate achieved by our algorithm (Section 4). Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our algorithm effectively detects shifts on ImageNet (Section 5).

**Sequential detection vs. sequential tests.** While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test, since the covariate shift may occur after a delay or gradually over time. A sequential test only applies if _all_ of the test data is shifted. Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps. This is necessary: we cannot guarantee that we detect a covariate shift occurring at a later point in time if we constrain the false positive to be bounded uniformly across all steps. In our experiments, we show that the rate of false alarms remains manageable while enabling our algorithm to detect covariate shift in a number of interesting scenarios.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Covariate shift.** There has been work on training models in the presence of covariate shift. In particular, in the unsupervised domain adaptation setting (Ben-David et al., 2007; Bickel et al., 2007; Ganin et al., 2016), the algorithm has access to labeled examples from the source domain but only unlabeled examples from the target domain, and the goal is to train a model that achieves good performance on the target domain. One strategy is to use importance weighting to upweight source examples that are more similar to target examples (Bickel et al., 2007). Another strategy is to first learn an invariant representation (Ganin et al., 2016), which is an embedding space where the source and target examples are similar, and then train a model on this embedding space using the source examples. If we detect covariate shift, one solution is to retrain the model using these techniques.

**Two-sample tests.** We focus on _classifier two-sample tests (C2ST)_ . In this approach, the idea is to train a binary classifier to distinguish source and target samples, compute a real-valued score based on this classifier as the test statistic, and then use a univariate two-sample test to determine the cutoff for rejecting the null hypothesis (Friedman, 2004). A natural test statistic is the classifier’s accuracy on a held-out test set (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), or the differences in the classifier’s logits (Cheng & Cloninger, 2019); in this work, we use the former. One way to compute the cutoff is to use the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017). Nonparametric tests such as permutation tests can also be used (Kim et al., 2021).

Another kind of two-sample test is called a _kernel two-sample test_ . In this approach, the idea is to use the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the two samples according to a given kernel embedding as the test statistic (Gretton et al., 2012a; Chwialkowski et al., 2015; Jitkrittum et al., 2016). The key design decision is the choice of kernel. One strategy is to use a nonparametric kernel such as Gaussian radial basis functions (Gretton et al., 2012a); alternatively, the kernel can also be optimized to minimize the false negative rate of the resulting test (Gretton et al., 2012b). In addition,

recent work has shown how to first learn a kernel function in the form of a deep neural network, and then evaluate the MMD distance on a held-out test set (Liu et al., 2020). The test statistic can be chosen based on finite sample bounds or based on its asymptotic distribution (Gretton et al., 2012a). Nonparametric permutation tests can also be used (Liu et al., 2020) with this approach.

**Concept Drift.** In the context of _concept drift_ (Gama et al., 2014), there has been work detecting shifts in _p_ ( _x, y_ ) (Gonc¸alves Jr et al., 2014). However, this work assumes that ground truth labels are provided for test examples, whereas our approach only requires unlabeled test examples. The former is substantially easier, since it suffices to check for drift in the distribution of prediction errors, which is usually very simple (e.g., a Bernoulli distribution for the 0-1 loss), making it easy to test for drift. In contrast, our approach checks for drift in high-dimensional covariates distribution.

**Sequential hypothesis testing.** A closely related problem is sequential hypothesis testing, which adaptively decides whether to reject the null hypothesis as samples become available (Wald, 1945). These approaches can also applied to two-sample testing (Balsubramani & Ramdas, 2015; Lh´eritier & Cazals, 2018; 2019; Manole & Ramdas, 2021). However, as discussed above, they assume that the each distribution of the two samples does not change over time. In contrast, we are interested in the setting where the test examples might initially be from the same distribution as the training examples, but then shift at a later point in time. Sequential tests are not applicable to this setting.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the effectiveness of our algorithm at detecting both natural and synthetic covariate shifts of varying forms (e.g., gradual shifts and multiple shifts back and forth), showing that it significantly outperforms natural baselines.

## 5.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Baselines.** We compare our algorithm to three baselines: two of them differ in the way they use the samples at each time step, the third uses Wald’s sequential likelihood test (Wald, 1945). For the first two baselines, while our approach uses all samples to construct the CP interval around the accuracy of the source-target classifier ˆ _gt_ as well as to train ˆ _gt_ , the baseline instead constructs a held-out test set using every _H[th]_ sample. Then, only this held-out test set is used to compute the CP interval, and only the remaining samples are used to train ˆ _gt_ . In our experiments, we used values of _H ∈{_ 2 _,_ 5 _}_ , denoted H2, H5, respectively. For Wald’s test, we consider the Bernoulli random variable with a probability _p_ indicating whether the prediction of the source-target classifier is correct for the given

This baseline is essentially the online version of an existing classifier two-sample test (C2ST) (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), which splits the (fixed) training dataset into a training set to train _g_ ˆ _t_ and a held-out test set to estimate the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ ; thus, _H_ controls the tradeoff between the number of examples in the training set and held-out test set.

**Source-target classifier.** We use a fully-connected neural network with a single hidden layer (with ˆ 128 hidden units) and with the ReLU activation functions as the source-target classifier _gt_ . We use a binary cross-entropy loss for training in conjunction with an SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01 (for natural shift experiments) and 0.001 (for synthetic shift experiments). Finally, since the inputs are ImageNet images (Russakovsky et al., 2015), we use a 2048-dimensional feature vector generated by first running a pretrained ResNet152 model (He et al., 2016) on the images, and then using these features vectors for the covariates of _Sw,t_ and _Tw,t_ .

**Scenarios.** We run each algorithm to test whether the target distribution in the given window is shifted with three different scenarios: multiple shift (“M-shift”), gradually increasing shift (“GIshift”), and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift (“GID-shift”). Table 1 describes each scenario. For example, the multiple shift scenario proceeds as follows: (i) it starts with no covariate shift at the beginning; (ii) after observing 25% target samples ( _i.e._ , 250 _[th]_ samples for natural shift experiments and 2500 _[th]_ samples for synthetic shift experiments), covariate shift is applied to all target samples (with probability 1) by adding random perturbations for synthetic shift and by drawing samples from a target distribution for natural shift; (iii) after 50% of target samples, it reverts to no covariate shift; and (iv) finally after observing 75% target samples, the covariate shift is applied to the all target samples. Gradually increasing shift and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift scenarios start with no covariate shift for the first 20% of target samples; then, covariate shift is applied with some probability 0 _< p <_ 1 by gradually changing _p_ over time.

**Stream data generation.** For each shift ( _i.e._ , natural shift and synthetic shift), we have a source dataset _S_ and target datasets _Tt_ , from which we randomly draw source and target samples for each time step _t_ . In particular, we consider a batch of samples for computational efficiency, where we denote the batch size by _B_ ; we use _B_ = 10 for our experiments. That is, we wait for _B_ samples to be collected from the target distribution before checking for covariate shift and the updating the sourcetarget classifier; then, we begin collecting the next batch. ## 5.2 NATURAL SHIFT

**Dataset.** First, we consider a natural shift on ImageNet. To construct such a shift, we consider the subset of dog classes; in particular, 120 of the 1000 of the ImageNet classes are of dogs (Khosla et al., 2011). Then, we randomly select half ( _i.e._ , 60) of these classes to be the source dataset, and the other half to be the target dataset; thus, the number of source and target images is 2997 each (after removing duplicated images). As a consequence, the source and target datasets correspond to different dog breeds, which is a kind of natural distribution shift.

**Results.** Figure 1 and Table 2 show results for the natural shift experiment with _w_ = 10 and _α_ = 1%. ## 6 CONCLUSION

We have proposed a novel covariate shift detection algorithm, which uses a classifier two-sample test to check whether the current test examples differ in distribution compared to the training examples. Our approach ensures sample efficiency by avoiding the need to split the dataset into a training set and a held-out test set, and instead using all the data to both train the source-target discriminator and to evaluate its accuracy. We prove that even with this optimization, our approach provides finite sample guarantees on the false positive rate at a desired level; we also prove bounds on the false negative rate under a mild conditions on the trained classifier. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our proposed algorithm is significantly more sample efficient compared to a natural baseline that uses a held-out test set in terms of detecting both natural and synthetic shifts on ImageNet.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to sequential covariate shift detection using classifier two-sample tests, with clear contributions in both algorithmic design and theoretical analysis. The problem setting—detecting distributional shifts in a streaming, online environment—is highly relevant to real-world deployment of machine learning systems, and the authors effectively position their work at the intersection of distributional robustness and statistical testing. The core idea of adapting classifier-based two-sample tests to an online learning framework by evaluating accuracy before each gradient update is both practical and novel, and the theoretical justification for maintaining finite-sample false positive rate control is a strong point of the paper. The clarity of exposition is generally high, with a logical flow from problem formulation to method to experiments. However, the most significant issue lies in the limited discussion of the assumptions underlying the classifier’s ability to provide nontrivial detection power—while the false negative analysis assumes the classifier achieves nontrivial accuracy, there is little exploration of how this depends on model capacity, data dimensionality, or the nature of the shift, which are critical for real-world applicability. Additionally, the distinction between "sequential covariate shift detection" and formal sequential hypothesis testing should be more clearly articulated early on, as the decision to forgo uniform Type I error control across time steps has important implications for deployment scenarios requiring sustained monitoring. Despite these concerns, the paper is well-written, technically rigorous, and offers a promising direction for practical distribution shift detection.

This appears to be a critical omission in the OCR or document rendering, but checking the surrounding context, no definition of Q_{w,t} appears in the provided passage.

From context, Q_{w,t} should be the mixture distribution that generates examples for the classifier test: with equal weight, sample from S or from T̄_{w,t}, and label accordingly (0 for S, 1 for T). This is standard in C2ST, and Lemma 2 later assumes that under H₀, distinguishing is impossible, implying balanced classes.

However, since the paper emphasizes finite-sample guarantees and the CP interval depends on the i.i.d. assumption from a fixed distribution, the formal definition of Q_{w,t} is necessary. Is it the uniform mixture over the current window samples? Or a distribution with density proportional to (S + T̄_{w,t})/2? The unbiasedness of μ̂_{w,t} depends on this.

The authors later treat the 2w predictions as i.i.d. Bernoulli(μ*_{w,t}), which requires that each (x_i, y_i) pair is drawn independently from Q_{w,t}. But without defining Q_{w,t}, and given that the classifier is updated online, it's unclear whether this holds.

While Algorithm 1 and Step 1 suggest that predictions are made on current and stored examples, the definition of Q_{w,t} must clarify whether it's defined over the streaming window and how labels are assigned. This omission leaves the theoretical foundation ambiguous.

Given that this definition is foundational for the statistical claims, and no forward reference (like 'see Section X') is provided, this constitutes a real underspecification.

comments.title: Incorrect null hypothesis in problem formulation
comments.quote: the null hypothesis is H_0 : S = T̄_{w,t}, and the alternative hypothesis is H_1 : S = T̄_{w,t}
comments.explanation:
In Section 3.1, the authors write: 'the null hypothesis is H_0 : S = T̄_{w,t}, and the alternative hypothesis is H_1 : S = T̄_{w,t}'. This is clearly a duplication—the alternative should be H_1: S ≠ T̄_{w,t}.

At first, this might be dismissed as a transcription or OCR error, but the text shows both hypotheses as equality. This is not a notation issue (e.g., typo in symbol) but a fundamental error in stating the testing framework.

Later, in Lemma 2 and Theorem 3, they correctly refer to the case when S = T̄_{w,t} as the null, and the test rejects when 0.5 is not in the CP interval, which aligns with H_1: S ≠ T̄_{w,t}. So the authors clearly intend the standard two-sample test setup.

However, explicitly writing H_1 as S = T̄_{w,t} contradicts basic hypothesis testing conventions and could mislead a careful reader, especially a student or researcher new to the area.

Given that the paper is under review and emphasizes theoretical correctness, such a basic definitional error in stating the hypotheses is unacceptable even if the rest of the paper corrects it implicitly. This is a logical inconsistency between the stated hypotheses and standard statistical practice.

comments.title: Use of future data in test statistic at time t
comments.quote: μ∗ w,t is the accuracy of ĝ at distinguishing whether an example x is from distribution S or T̄_{w,t}
comments.explanation:
The definition of μ*_{w,t} depends on T̄_{w,t} = (1/w) ∑_{k=t−w+1}^t T_k, which averages target distributions from time t−w+1 to t. However, at time step t, the algorithm uses predictions from times t−w+1 to t to compute μ̂_{w,t}. The issue is whether the classifier ĝ_t used at time t to make these past predictions is the same as the one that was available at those times.

The algorithm in Step 1 says: 'predict on current samples xt and x′t using the current classifier ĝ_t'. But for computing μ̂_{w,t}, the empirical accuracy over the window, the authors must be using predictions made by ĝ_{t−w+1}, ..., ĝ_t on the corresponding samples. But the algorithm only stores or uses the current classifier.

Looking at Algorithm 1: at time t, it uses ĝ_t to predict on xt and x′t, then updates ĝ_t. There is no indication that past classifiers ĝ_{t−1}, etc., are stored. Therefore, to compute μ̂_{w,t}, the algorithm must be re-using the current ĝ_t to predict on past samples (xt−w+1, ..., xt) and (x′t−w+1, ..., x′t). But these past samples were not used i.i.d. from Q_{w,t} with respect to the current ĝ_t—they were used to train previous versions of the classifier.

This breaks the independence assumption required for the CP interval. Lemma 1 claims conditional independence of predictions given past classifiers, but if we are applying the current ĝ_t to past data that it may have been trained on (directly or indirectly), then the predictions are not independent.

The only way to salvage this is if the window of data S_{w,t} and T_{w,t} is stored, and at each time t, the current classifier ĝ_t is applied to all 2w stored examples to compute μ̂_{w,t}. But even then, many of those examples were used to train ĝ_t (via past update steps), so the predictions are not fresh test samples.

The key claim in Step 2 is that μ̂_{w,t} is an 'unbiased empirical estimate', but if it's computed on data used for training, it's optimistic and biased.

The method section does not specify whether the predictions used for μ̂_{w,t} are the ones made in real-time (and stored) or recomputed at each step with the current model. The former would require storing 2w predictions per step; the latter introduces data reuse.

Given that the false positive control relies on the CP interval assuming independent test samples, and the training-update loop uses the same data, this is a major concern unless explicitly addressed. The algorithm as described does not prevent data contamination.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for accuracy estimator
comments.quote: The unbiased empirical estimate of this accuracy is denoted by [empty]
comments.explanation:
The sentence 'The unbiased empirical estimate of this accuracy is denoted by' is followed by no equation or symbol. This appears to be a rendering or OCR error where the definition of μ̂_{w,t} is missing.

From context, μ̂_{w,t} is later used, so it must be defined as the average over the 2w predictions in the window. But without its explicit definition, we cannot verify if it's computed over real-time predictions or re-evaluated ones.

The paper summary defines μ_{w,t} as the empirical accuracy, so the symbol is established. But in the passage, the lack of definition—especially right after introducing μ*_{w,t}—creates ambiguity.

This is not just a missing equation: the claim that it is 'unbiased' depends entirely on when and how the predictions are generated. If the prediction on (xt,0) is made before updating the classifier on (xt,0), and stored, then it can be unbiased. But the text does not explicitly state that the predictions from Step 1 are stored and reused for μ̂_{w,t}.

The algorithm computes a detection decision at every step, so μ̂_{w,t} must be computed at each t. Without knowing whether it uses stored past predictions or re-runs the current model, the reproducibility and statistical validity are in question.

This is an underspecification that directly impacts the validity of Lemma 1 and Theorem 3.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of false negative rate bound condition
comments.quote: If S = Tw,t and w − 1 −⌊ ~~~~ w log(2 /α ) ⌋≥ 0 , then we have
comments.explanation: The condition in Theorem 5 is stated as 'w^{-1} - ⌊ w log(2/α) ⌋ ≥ 0' in the PAPER SUMMARY, but in the passage it appears as 'w − 1 − ⌊ w log(2 / α) ⌋ ≥ 0'. The passage version seems to interpret this as (w - 1) minus the floor term, which is very different from w^{-1} (i.e., 1/w). Given that w is a window size, likely in the dozens or hundreds, 1/w is a small positive number (e.g., 0.01 for w=100), but w - 1 is large. Furthermore, the floor of w log(2/α) for α=0.01 is about w * 5.3, so w-1 - floor(w log(2/α)) would be negative for all w ≥ 1, contradicting the claim that the bound holds for w ≥ 201. This suggests a LaTeX/OCR error where w^{-1} was misread as w - 1. The intended expression is almost certainly w^{-1} - ⌊ √(w log(2/α)) ⌋ ≥ 0 (with a square root), as this matches common forms in concentration inequalities. The text later says the condition suggests w ≥ 201 when α=0.01, which only makes sense if the term involves √(w log(2/α)) — without the square root, the expression grows with w and cannot be non-negative for large w. Even with context, the presence of this condition in two different forms (in summary and in body) and the implausibility of w-1 - floor(w log(2/α)) ≥ 0 indicates a real error. This affects the validity of Theorem 5's assumptions and undercuts the false negative analysis.

comments.title: Ambiguous assumption on classifier inaccuracy with inconsistent notation
comments.quote: We note that the assumption L (ˆ gt ) := 1 − µ[∗] ≤ 1 / 2 − ϵ can be achieved under standard conditions. For instance, assume that the model family G of source-target classifiers has finite VC dimension ( i.e. , VC( G ) < ∞ ), and that the optimal model g[∗] ∈G has nontrivial inaccuracy L ( g[∗] ) = 1 / 2 − ξ for some ξ ∈ R > 0
comments.explanation: The passage defines L(ĝ_t) := 1 - μ*_{w,t} ≤ 1/2 - ϵ, implying that the inaccuracy is at most 1/2 - ϵ, i.e., accuracy μ*_{w,t} ≥ 1/2 + ϵ. But then it says the optimal model g* has inaccuracy L(g*) = 1/2 - ξ, which would mean accuracy 1/2 + ξ — consistent with the requirement. However, the phrasing 'has nontrivial inaccuracy' suggests that 1/2 - ξ is less than 1/2, meaning ξ > 0, which is correct. The issue arises when the passage says this implies L(ĝ_t) ≤ 1/2 - ϵ, but the generalization bound from VC theory would ensure that the empirical risk minimizer has inaccuracy close to that of g*, not better. So L(ĝ_t) ≈ 1/2 - ξ, not necessarily ≤ 1/2 - ϵ unless ϵ ≤ ξ. The passage then says 'the second term (which we have taken to be ϵ) satisfies ϵ > 0 for sufficiently large m', suggesting ϵ is derived from the generalization gap. But in Theorem 5, ϵ is a fixed lower bound on how much better than random the classifier is. So the conflation of ϵ as both a problem parameter and a quantity derived from data size m is confusing. The authors are trying to justify that μ*_{w,t} ≥ 1/2 + ϵ can be achieved, but the use of the same ϵ in both the assumption and the generalization conclusion risks circularity. A more careful treatment would distinguish the inherent distinguishability gap ξ from the achievable gap ϵ after generalization.

comments.title: Misleading claim about dominance of term in false negative bound
comments.quote: In the false negative bound, the first term is dominant and increases as w increases
comments.explanation: The false negative rate bound in Theorem 5 is of the form exp(−2w(ϵ − √(log(2/α)/(4w)))^2 ). This expression depends on w in a non-monotonic way. For small w, the term √(log(2/α)/(4w)) is large, possibly larger than ϵ, making the exponent positive and the bound close to 1. As w increases, this term decreases, so (ϵ - small)^2 increases, making the exponent more negative and the bound decrease — i.e., false negative rate decreases with w, which is good. So the statement that 'the first term is dominant and increases as w increases' is incorrect. The bound itself decreases (improves) with larger w, not increases. The authors may have meant that the sample size must increase to make the bound non-vacuous, which is true, but saying the bound 'increases' with w is misleading and contradicts standard intuition in statistical learning: more samples should improve detection power. This overstatement could mislead readers about how the detector scales with window size.

comments.title: Underspecified batch processing in algorithm description
comments.quote: we wait for B samples to be collected from the target distribution before checking for covariate shift and the updating the source-target classifier
comments.explanation: The core algorithm (Algorithm 1) is described in a per-step fashion (for each time step t), but in the experiment section, it is revealed that the implementation actually processes samples in batches of size B=10. However, it is not specified how the statistics (e.g., μ̂_{w,t}) are updated within a batch — whether predictions are accumulated, whether the classifier is updated incrementally within the batch, or whether the batch is treated as a block. This affects both reproducibility and theoretical validity: the conditional independence in Lemma 1 assumes one sample at a time is used before update. Processing a batch of B samples would require B predictions with the same classifier before B updates, which could preserve conditional independence given the past classifier, but this needs to be explicitly stated. The current description leaves ambiguous whether the batch is used for detection before any updates (as in Algorithm 1) or if multiple predictions are made before detection. This is a real gap in method specification.

comments.title: Incorrect statement of Wald test null and alternative hypotheses
comments.quote: The hypothesis test is H0 : p = 0.5 vs. H1 : p = 0.5+ ϵ , where ϵ = 0.2
comments.explanation: The Wald sequential test is a parametric sequential probability ratio test (SPRT) that requires fully specified point hypotheses under H0 and H1. However, in practice, the alternative hypothesis H1: p = 0.7 (0.5 + 0.2) may not reflect the true accuracy of the classifier under shift, especially if the detector's power depends on the actual distinguishability. More importantly, the Wald test as described does not align with how it would be applied to detect arbitrary covariate shifts — the choice of ϵ = 0.2 is arbitrary and not justified. Furthermore, if the true accuracy under shift is less than 0.7, the test may be underpowered; if it's higher, it may stop too early but with correct detection. The bigger issue is that the paper presents Wald's test as a baseline for detecting general shift, but Wald's test is designed for detection of a specific, known shift size. This makes the comparison potentially unfair: our method is non-parametric and detects any deviation from 1/2, while the Wald test is tuned for a specific p=0.7. This understates the generality of the proposed method and overstates the capability of the baseline.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in Clopper-Pearson interval argument
comments.quote: ΘCP(2 wµ̂ w,t, 2 w ; α)
comments.explanation: The binomial parameter passed to ΘCP is written as 2wμ̂_{w,t}, which is correct because μ̂_{w,t} is average accuracy over 2w samples (w source and w target), so 2wμ̂_{w,t} is the count of correct predictions, an integer between 0 and 2w. This matches the definition s = n · μ̂ where n=2w. However, in the text it is written as '2 wµw,t,' with an overscript hat missing on µ in one instance (e.g., '2 wµw,t,' vs '2wµ̂_{w,t}'). In the passage, it appears as '2 wµw,t,' which lacks the hat. Given that µ̂_{w,t} is the empirical accuracy, this is likely a typo or OCR error. The surrounding context consistently uses hat notation, so this is probably not a conceptual issue. Since the correct symbol is used elsewhere and the meaning is clear, this is minor.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation between $ L(\hat{g}_t) $ and claimed nontrivial inaccuracy condition
comments.quote: the assumption $ L(\hat{g}_t) := 1 - \mu^*_{w,t} \le 1/2 - \epsilon $ can be achieved under standard conditions. For instance, assume that the model family $ G $ of source-target classifiers has finite VC dimension (i.e., VC( $ G $ ) < $ \infty $), and that the optimal model $ g^* \in G $ has nontrivial inaccuracy $ L(g^*) = 1/2 - \xi $ for some $ \xi \in \mathbb{R} > 0 $
comments.explanation: At first glance, the paper defines $ L(\hat{g}_t) = 1 - \mu^*_{w,t} $ as the inaccuracy of the current classifier, which aligns with standard usage. The authors later state a sufficient condition for their false negative bound: that $ L(\hat{g}_t) \le 1/2 - \epsilon $. However, they justify this by appealing to the existence of an optimal model $ g^* $ with $ L(g^*) = 1/2 - \xi $, implying that $ \mu^*_{w,t} \ge 1/2 + \xi $ (since $ \mu^*_{w,t} $ is accuracy). But this does not automatically imply that the learned $ \hat{g}_t $ achieves $ \mu^*_{w,t} \ge 1/2 + \epsilon $—a gap exists between population-level optimal performance and finite-sample classifier accuracy. The claim that the condition $ L(\hat{g}_t) \le 1/2 - \epsilon $ holds under finite VC dimension and existence of a nontrivial $ g^* $ is plausible but requires a generalization bound to close the gap. While such bounds are standard in learning theory, the authors omit reference to one (e.g., VC or Rademacher-based), making the justification feel underspecified. The leap from $ L(g^*) = 1/2 - \xi $ to $ L(\hat{g}_t) \le 1/2 - \epsilon $ is nontrivial and should be supported by a probabilistic generalization argument involving $ m = 2w $. Without it, the justification appears hand-wavy.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed FPR control and table values exceeding $ \alpha = 0.01 $
comments.quote: In Table 3b, we bold values that exceed the desired $ \alpha = 1\% $. ... H2: 1.00, 0.90, 0.80, 0.73 at increasing sample sizes under M-shift
comments.explanation: The paper claims in multiple places (e.g., in the discussion of both natural and synthetic shifts) that 'all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound (i.e., FPR $ \le \alpha $)'. However, Table 3b explicitly indicates that values exceeding $ \alpha = 1\% $ are bolded, and several entries—for example, H2 and H5 under M-shift at 500 samples—are above 1% (e.g., 1.00%, 0.90%). While these are slightly above 1%, they are flagged by the authors themselves as exceeding the threshold. This directly contradicts the textual claim that all methods satisfy the FPR bound. The claim may be intended to apply only to the proposed method (ours), but the wording clearly includes 'all algorithms'. Given that the baselines use held-out sets and Wald test with CP or asymptotic thresholds, it's plausible they don't uniformly achieve nominal FPR. The issue lies not with the data but with the overstatement in the prose. This undermines confidence in the fairness of the comparison and the correctness of claimed guarantees.

comments.title: Ambiguity in use of $ \epsilon $: conflating effect size and accuracy gap
comments.quote: the assumption $ \mu^*_{w,t} \ge 1/2 + \epsilon $, where $ \epsilon \in (0, 1/2] $, is the accuracy at distinguishing $ S $ and $ \bar{T}_{w,t} $ ... $ \epsilon = 0.2 $: effect size in Wald test under $ H_1 $
comments.explanation: The symbol $ \epsilon $ is used in two distinct contexts: (1) as a lower bound on the gap between classifier accuracy and 1/2 (i.e., signal strength in the C2ST), and (2) as the fixed effect size in the Wald test baseline ($ H_1: p = 0.5 + \epsilon $ with $ \epsilon = 0.2 $). While mathematically both represent deviations from 0.5, conflating the notation is confusing, especially because in Theorem 5, $ \epsilon $ is treated as a property of the learned classifier, while in the Wald test, it is a user-specified parameter. The authors should have used distinct symbols (e.g., $ \delta $ for the Wald effect size) to avoid confusion. Although readers can disambiguate from context, the reuse of $ \epsilon $ across different statistical roles—especially when one method's performance depends on an unknown $ \epsilon $ while another fixes it—risks misinterpretation of the comparison. This is particularly problematic when assessing 'sample efficiency', as the Wald test’s fixed $ \epsilon $ may not match the actual signal strength.

comments.title: Unclear how Wald test baseline is applied in time-varying shift scenarios
comments.quote: baselines use held-out test sets (H2, H5) or Wald’s sequential test with fixed effect size $ \epsilon = 0.2 $
comments.explanation: The Wald sequential test is designed for testing a persistent shift under $ H_1: p = 0.5 + \epsilon $, but the evaluated scenarios include M-shift (multiple shifts), GI-shift (gradually increasing), and GID-shift (increasing then decreasing). These involve temporary or non-stationary deviations from the null. It's unclear how the Wald test is adapted to such settings—does it restart after each decision? Is it reset periodically? The paper states in Section 4 that 'Wald test is restarted after each decision', but this is buried in the 'Assumptions' list and not discussed in the baseline description. Without this detail, the Wald test’s ability to detect transient or non-monotonic shifts is questionable. Furthermore, the Wald test’s false positive rate is only controlled over the entire sequence under independence and stationarity, which may not hold here. The lack of clarity on implementation makes it difficult to interpret why Wald underperforms (or fails to report detection times in tables), raising concerns about fairness in comparison.

comments.title: Missing clarification on why Wald entries are empty in Table 3a
comments.quote:
Wald
-
-
-
-
-
comments.explanation: In Table 3a, the Wald method has no reported values (marked with '-') across all scenarios and perturbations. The text does not explain why. Given that Wald is a baseline, its absence prevents comparison on sample efficiency. Possible reasons include: failure to detect shift within the time limit, incompatibility with the windowed setup, or computational constraints. However, none are stated. This undermines the completeness of the empirical evaluation. While the authors may assume the reason is obvious (e.g., Wald requires longer sequences), in a paper emphasizing sample efficiency, omitting an explanation for a baseline's failure to produce results is a significant oversight. A brief note in the caption or text would suffice to maintain transparency.

comments.title: Incorrect Hoeffding bound expression in Theorem 5
comments.explanation: The expressions for a(w, α) and b(w, α) appear to involve a square root symbol that was corrupted by OCR (rendered as ). I initially suspected the intent was to write the Hoeffding bound of the form 1/2 ± sqrt(log(2/α)/(2n)), scaled appropriately. Given that n = 2w in this context, the deviation term should be ~sqrt(log(2/α)/(4w)). However, the expression inside the parentheses is written as ± log(2/α)/(4w) — without the square root. This is inconsistent with standard Hoeffding bounds, which scale with the square root of the confidence term over sample size. The presence of 'log' strongly suggests an OCR failure where \sqrt{\log} became log. Assuming the intended expression was 1/2 ± \sqrt{\log(2/α)/(4w)}, then multiplying by 2w gives a binomial count bound of w ± \sqrt{w \log(2/α)/4}, which aligns better with typical forms. However, even that would need careful scaling. The current expression as rendered lacks the square root, implying a deviation decaying only as 1/w, which is far too optimistic and would lead to incorrect false negative bounds. While the surrounding context in Theorem 5 suggests this bound is derived from Hoeffding (citing Lemma 4), the formula as written corresponds to an incorrect concentration inequality. This is not just a notation issue: using the logarithm without the square root would invalidate the bound on the false negative rate. Given that Lemma 4 claims the CP interval is narrower than a Hoeffding-type interval, the derivation hinges on correct scaling. The error, if not due to OCR, undermines the theoretical false negative rate claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent null and alternative hypotheses in Section 3.1
comments.quote: the null hypothesis is H 0 : S = T¯ w,t , and the alternative hypothesis is H 1 : S = T¯ w,t
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears to be a clear typographical error — both H0 and H1 are written as S = T¯ w,t, which is logically incoherent. The surrounding text establishes that the goal is to detect when a shift occurs, so H0 should be 'no shift' (S = T¯ w,t) and H1 should be 'shift' (S ≠ T¯ w,t). The repetition of the equality suggests either an OCR error or a copy-paste mistake during drafting. The context of the paper—especially the detection rule based on whether 1/2 is in the CP interval—confirms that the alternative should represent a difference. This contradicts standard hypothesis testing conventions and misstates the fundamental statistical setup. While the rest of the paper clearly operates under the correct interpretation (e.g., false positives occur under H0), a careful reader might waste time verifying whether this was a deep conceptual error. Since hypothesis definition is foundational, any ambiguity here is significant. However, given that the algorithm and analysis elsewhere are consistent with the proper formulation, and assuming this is a mechanical error, the impact is mitigated.

comments.title: Missing definition of T¯ w,t in formal notation
comments.quote: where T̄ w,t =[] k = t−w +1 T k /w , i.e., whether the average target distributions over the previous w steps is shifted compared to S
comments.explanation: The definition of T̄ w,t = [] k = t−w+1 T_k / w appears to suffer from OCR corruption ( instead of a summation symbol). While the prose says 'average target distributions', the mathematical expression is ambiguous: it's unclear whether this is a mixture distribution, an average of parameters, or an empirical distribution. In the context of two-sample testing, we typically compare empirical samples from S and from the windowed target T'_{t−w+1:t}. The notation suggests an average over distributions T_k, but T_k is not defined as a distribution — earlier it's used as a realization x′_t ∼ T_t. This creates confusion: is T̄_w,t a mixture of w different distributions? Or is it simply the empirical distribution of the last w target samples? Given that the algorithm uses (x′_{t−w+1}, ..., x′_t), it is almost certainly the latter. However, the formal definition mixes distributional averaging with sequential realization. This ambiguity affects reproducibility: a reader might misunderstand whether the method assumes a fixed shift or allows time-varying targets. The correct interpretation—comparing S to the empirical distribution of the last w target samples—is recoverable from Algorithm 1 and the use of µ̂_w,t over 2w samples—but only with effort. A precise definition like T̄_w,t := Uniform({x′_{t−w+1}, ..., x′_t}) would eliminate this. As written, it risks miscommunication about the test's operating regime.

comments.title: Unjustified use of Clopper-Pearson interval with dependent predictions
comments.quote: the accuracy μ̂_w,t is the parameter of the Binomial distribution
comments.explanation: The paper claims that 2wμ̂_w,t follows a Binomial(2w, μ*_w,t) distribution and justifies this with Lemma 1, which asserts conditional independence of predictions given past classifiers. At first, this seemed resolved by the lemma. However, upon deeper inspection, the Binomial model requires not only independence but identical distribution (i.i.d.) of the Bernoulli trials z_i = 1(ĝ_t(x_i) = y_i). While Lemma 1 claims conditional independence, it does not establish that the z_i are identically distributed. In fact, because ĝ_t is updated online, the distribution of each z_i depends on the training history up to i−1, meaning the success probability of each trial varies — so z_i ~ Bernoulli(p_i) with p_i ≠ p_j in general. Therefore, the sum is not Binomial(2w, μ*), but a Poisson Binomial, which has different variance and tail behavior. The Clopper-Pearson interval assumes i.i.d. Bernoulli trials; applying it to non-identical trials can invalidate the coverage guarantee. The authors acknowledge dependence but only address independence—not identical distribution. While Lemma 1 helps, it does not fully justify the use of CP intervals. This is a nontrivial statistical point: finite-sample binomial confidence intervals do not generally apply to sums of independent but non-identical Bernoulli variables without correction. The paper does not discuss this limitation, nor provide adjusted bounds. This undermines the claimed FPR guarantee in Theorem 3, which depends on exact CP coverage.

comments.title: Ambiguous scope of Theorem 3 and false positive rate claim
comments.quote: If S = T̄ w,t, then for any ĝ_t, we have P[1/2 ∉ Θ_CP(2wμ̂_w,t, 2w; α)] ≤ α
comments.explanation: At first, this appears to correctly state that under the null, the false positive rate is controlled at level α. The paper claims 'All algorithms satisfy FPR ≤ α = 1%', and Theorem 3 seems to justify this. However, the theorem is stated for a single time step t. In a sequential setting where the test is run repeatedly, the concern is whether the per-step FPR or the family-wise error rate (FWER) is controlled. The abstract and introduction suggest that the bound holds 'across all scenarios', but Theorem 3 only provides per-step control. The 'Sequential detection vs. sequential tests' section acknowledges this distinction, stating 'the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps'. So the theorem itself is correctly stated. However, the empirical evaluation claims to measure FPR at fixed sample counts (500, 1000, ...) under no-shift conditions — which could be interpreted as evaluating cumulative false alarm rate over time. If multiple tests are performed up to sample 2000 with window w=10, there are ~200 opportunities for false alarms, so even α=1% per step could lead to high cumulative error. The paper says FPR is computed 'under no-shift conditions', but does not specify whether it's the probability of at least one false detection or the per-window false alarm rate. This ambiguity matters: if 'FPR' in the experiments refers to cumulative error, then the theoretical per-step bound does not ensure FPR ≤ 1%. The connection between theory and experiment is underspecified, which could mislead readers about the strength of the guarantee.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated approach to the pervasive problem of covariate shift in streaming data, addressing a clear practical gap by moving from static classifier-based two-sample tests (C2ST) to an online, sequential learning setting. The introduction is structurally strong, effectively balancing the theoretical motivation (the use of Clopper-Pearson intervals to avoid asymptotic approximations) with the pragmatic challenges of real-time application (computational constraints and sample efficiency). However, the paper faces a significant potential hurdle in its "Sequential detection vs. sequential tests" discussion: the author acknowledges that the false positive rate (FPR) bound is only valid per-step rather than uniformly across time. Given this, the manuscript must be exceptionally rigorous in demonstrating how this choice avoids "false discovery" spirals in continuous deployment; if the empirical section does not address the cumulative risk of false positives over long time horizons, the proposed method may be viewed as academically interesting but practically unreliable for high-stakes environments like autonomous driving. Addressing this potential "multiple testing" problem more explicitly in the theoretical framing would greatly strengthen the paper’s contribution.

comments.title: Inconsistency in CP interval definition notation
comments.quote:
where _F_ ( _s_ ; _n, θ_ ) is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of Binomial( _n, θ_ ). To compute the CP interval, we can use the following equivalent formula:

    [section 2] where _Q_ ( _p, a, b_ ) is the _p_ th quantile of a Beta distribution with parameters _a, b_
comments.explanation: The text states the Clopper-Pearson interval can be computed using the $p$-th quantile of a Beta distribution, but it omits the actual formula (the relationship between the Binomial CDF and the Beta distribution). While standard in the literature, the sentence preceding the missing equation leads the reader to expect an explicit formula involving $Q(p, a, b)$. This creates a minor discontinuity where the reader must bridge the gap via their own knowledge of the identity $BinomialCDF(s; n, \theta) = 1 - I_\theta(s+1, n-s)$, which relates to the Beta distribution $Beta(s+1, n-s)$. The transition is slightly abrupt.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of null hypothesis rejection threshold
comments.quote: our algorithm returns “covariate shift” if 1 _/_ 2 _̸∈_ ΘCP(2 _wµ_ ˆ _w,t,_ 2 _w_ ; _α_ )
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the paper defines the accuracy $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ as the mean of a Bernoulli variable based on $w$ samples. In Section 3.2, it introduces the term $2w \hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ as the Binomial random variable. However, it is unclear if the test uses $w$ samples or $2w$ samples. Algorithm 1, Step 5, uses $2w$ as the number of trials with estimated mean $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$, but the text implies the window size is $w$. If the sliding window contains $w$ source and $w$ target samples, there are effectively $2w$ total samples; however, the definition of the window $w$ in section 3.1 ($Sw,t = (x_{t-w+1}, ... x_t)$) seems to imply $w$ samples per distribution. The shift between $w$ and $2w$ in the arguments of $\Theta_{CP}$ is potentially confusing without a explicit clarification on the total sample budget per test iteration.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding false positive rate bounds
comments.quote: Thus, our algorithm is an effective strategy for sequential covariate shift detection.
comments.explanation: The authors claim in the Introduction that they do not use sequential hypothesis tests because shifts may be gradual, and then note that 'the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps'. While correct, referring to this as 'effective' for sequential detection is a strong claim. In sequential analysis, a 'per-step' bound does not control the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER) or False Discovery Rate (FDR) over a stream. A reader might be misled into thinking this provides a rigorous sequential guarantee, whereas it is actually a sequence of independent 'memoryless' tests that will inevitably trigger false positives at a rate of at least $\alpha$ per timestep.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in false negative bound condition
comments.quote: If S = Tw,t and w − 1 −⌊ 2w log(2 /α) ⌋ >= 0, then we have
comments.explanation: In Section 4, Theorem 5, the condition provided for the validity of the bound is `w − 1 − ⌊ 2w log(2 /α) ⌋ >= 0`. However, the definition of `a(w, α)` and `b(w, α)` given in the same theorem uses `2w(1/2 ± sqrt(log(2/α) / 2w))`. The square root term inside the floor function in the condition (which appears to be `⌊ sqrt(2w log(2/α)) ⌋` or similar based on standard CP interval derivations) is missing the radical sign in the printed text. Furthermore, the expression `2w log(2 /α)` inside the floor function would grow linearly with `w`, making the inequality `w - 1 - 2w log(2/α) >= 0` impossible to satisfy for large `w` and standard `α`. This is likely an OCR or transcription error, but as written, it is mathematically nonsensical.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the second term in Theorem 5
comments.quote: where the second term (which we have taken to be ϵ) satisfies ϵ > 0 for sufficiently large m.
comments.explanation: The text references a 'second term' in the inequality derived from VC dimension bounds for the false negative rate, but the inequality itself is missing from the provided text block. While the paper mentions that the second term is 'ϵ', it does not clarify if this ϵ refers to the same ϵ defined earlier in the theorem (`μ*w,t >= 1/2 + ϵ`). If the second term represents an estimation error (like a confidence interval width), setting it equal to the effect size ϵ without explicit derivation or bounds (e.g., in terms of δ and VC dimension) creates a circular definition that makes the claim about the Bound on the False Negative Rate difficult to verify.

comments.title: Inconsistency in boundary condition for w
comments.quote: the condition on w suggests that the bound is valid when w ≥ 201 given α = 0.01.
comments.explanation: Given the formula `a(w, α) := 2w(1/2 + sqrt(log(2/α) / 4w))`, if we let `α = 0.01`, then `log(2/0.01) = log(200) ≈ 5.3`. The condition for nontriviality often relates to the width of the confidence interval being less than the effect size. If the condition for the bound validity were `sqrt(log(2/α) / 4w) < 1/2`, then `log(200) / 4w < 1/4`, which implies `5.3/w < 1` or `w > 5.3`. The claim that `w >= 201` is required for `α = 0.01` is inconsistent with the provided formula unless the error term in the theorem involves a much stricter constraint (e.g., requiring the interval width to be a specific fraction of ϵ). The gap between the mathematical expression and the cited numeric threshold (201) is not justified.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table 3b FPR notation
comments.quote: In (b), we bold values that exceed the desired $\alpha = 1\%$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1 and Theorem 3, the authors define $\alpha$ as the significance level for the false positive rate, stating the goal is $P(\text{Reject } H_0) \le \alpha$. In Table 3b, the authors present FPR percentages (e.g., 0.73, 0.40). If $\alpha = 1\%$, then any value greater than 1.0 (or 1 in a decimal representation) would exceed the bound. However, the table contains values like 0.73, 0.89, etc., and the text says 'we bold values that exceed the desired $\alpha = 1\%$.' Yet, none of the values in the table exceed 1.0. This makes the intended threshold for bolding ambiguous or mathematically incorrect based on the provided table data.

comments.explanation: Table 3a and 3b show 'Wald' entries as '-' (dashes), implying the algorithm failed to return valid detection samples or FPR data. While the paper mentions Wald's sequential likelihood test in the introduction and Section 2, it provides no explanation as to why it is entirely excluded from the quantitative results (not even a baseline zero or failure code). comments.title: Ambiguity in 'detector' definition versus 'shift detection rate'
comments.quote: our approach always detects covariate shift at a rate higher than 80%.
comments.explanation: The text switches between detecting a shift 'at any time t' and measuring 'detection rate' (over repetitions). The problem formulation in 3.1 sets up a binary hypothesis test at each step $t$, but then claims to detect shift at a 'rate' of 80% (implying power). It is unclear if this 'rate' refers to the frequency of correct rejections across Monte Carlo trials at a fixed time $t$ after the shift occurs, or if it is a time-to-detection metric. Given the phrasing 'number of shifted samples required to reach at least 80%', it appears to be a power requirement, but the distinction between the per-step binary detector and the 'rate' could be clarified to avoid confusion with the FPR bound.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Null Hypothesis Definition
comments.quote: In particular, our algorithm returns “covariate shift” if $1/2 \notin \Theta_{CP}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}, 2w; \alpha)$, and “no covariate shift” otherwise
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the null hypothesis is defined as $H_0: S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$. Lemma 2 states that if $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$, then $\mu^*_{w,t} = 1/2$. The CP interval $\Theta_{CP}$ is an interval estimate of the true accuracy $\mu^*_{w,t}$. The rejection condition $1/2 \notin \Theta_{CP}$ correctly tests whether the true accuracy differs from 1/2. However, there is a technical ambiguity: $\Theta_{CP}$ is an interval for the parameter $\mu^*$, but the condition $1/2 \notin \Theta_{CP}$ implies we are rejecting the null hypothesis $S=T$. The notation $2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ inside $\Theta_{CP}$ is slightly inconsistent with standard CP notation where the interval is usually a function of the number of successes ($s$) and total trials ($n$). While $s = 2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$, the inclusion of the factor $2w$ in the first argument of the function $\Theta_{CP}$ defined in the text (which takes $(s, n)$) is redundant or potentially misleading as $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ is already the empirical mean. This is likely an OCR misread or a notation slip.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding False Negative rate bound
comments.quote: If $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$ and $w - 1 - \lfloor \dots \rfloor \geq 0$, then we have...
comments.explanation: The condition for the False Negative theorem (Theorem 5) starts with 'If $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$'. However, the context of a False Negative rate requires the alternative hypothesis to be true ($S \neq \bar{T}_{w,t}$) to evaluate the power of the test. The condition $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$ is the Null hypothesis. This is a likely typographical error in the paper where the authors meant $S \neq \bar{T}_{w,t}$ or a condition involving $\mu^* > 1/2 + \epsilon$.

comments.title: Mismatch in Theorem 5 condition
comments.quote: If $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$ and $w - 1 - \lfloor \dots \rfloor \geq 0$, then we have...
comments.explanation: Further analyzing Theorem 5: the bound is meant to be on the probability of not rejecting the null (False Negative). If the condition were $S = \bar{T}_{w,t}$, the probability of rejection is bounded by $\alpha$ (Type I error). For a False Negative bound, one must assume the alternative holds. The LaTeX fragment provided as the condition for the Theorem seems to copy the Null case. This undermines the mathematical validity of the FN rate bound section.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper presents a well-motivated approach to the pervasive problem of covariate shift in streaming data, addressing a clear practical gap by moving from static classifier-based two-sample tests (C2ST) to an online, sequential learning setting. The introduction is structurally strong, effectively balancing the theoretical motivation (the use of Clopper-Pearson intervals to avoid asymptotic approximations) with the pragmatic challenges of real-time application (computational constraints and sample efficiency). However, the paper faces a significant potential hurdle in its "Sequential detection vs. sequential tests" discussion: the author acknowledges that the false positive rate (FPR) bound is only valid per-step rather than uniformly across time. Given this, the manuscript must be exceptionally rigorous in demonstrating how this choice avoids "false discovery" spirals in continuous deployment; if the empirical section does not address the cumulative risk of false positives over long time horizons, the proposed method may be viewed as academically interesting but practically unreliable for high-stakes environments like autonomous driving. Addressing this potential "multiple testing" problem more explicitly in the theoretical framing would greatly strengthen the paper’s contribution.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-level Feedback

This paper addresses a practically important problem—sequential covariate shift detection—and proposes a thoughtful adaptation of classifier two-sample tests to the online setting. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, particularly in the introduction and motivation, where the challenges of sequential data and multiple shift events are well-articulated. However, the paper suffers from a significant disconnect between its stated ambitions and its theoretical and empirical contributions. The authors claim to handle "multiple" shifts (e.g., shift, then shift back) but provide no theoretical guarantees for the false negative rate (FNR) under such non-monotonic shifts, and the FNR bounds presented in Section 4 are stated "under mild conditions" that are not verified or discussed in the context of realistic, complex data (e.g., ImageNet). Furthermore, the empirical evaluation, while benchmarked on ImageNet, appears to lack comparisons to baselines that are explicitly designed for sequential or online shift detection (e.g., sequential probability ratio tests, online MMD with sliding windows), making it unclear how the proposed method compares to existing alternatives in the sequential setting. The most critical issue is the paper's treatment of false positive control: the authors explicitly state they do not use a sequential test and only guarantee per-step FPR, yet this limitation is under-discussed relative to the practical need for low cumulative false alarms over long deployment horizons. Without a more rigorous treatment of sequential testing theory or an empirical study of cumulative FPR, the practical utility of the method remains questionable. The paper would also benefit from a clearer explanation of why online gradient steps preserve test validity—this is a key technical claim that is currently undersupported. Overall, while the core idea is promising and the writing is clear, the paper's contributions are not yet fully substantiated by its theory or experiments, and major revisions are needed to close the gap between the claimed generality and the provided evidence.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'sequential test'
comments.quote: We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold.
comments.explanation: The introduction mentions 'the sequential test' without defining it. Later, in the 'Sequential detection vs. sequential tests' paragraph (still in the provided passage), the authors explicitly state that they deliberately choose not to use a sequential hypothesis test. This creates ambiguity: a reader encountering the earlier sentence could reasonably interpret 'sequential test' as a formal sequential hypothesis test (e.g., Wald's sequential probability ratio test), which would conflict with the later clarification. The context does not immediately resolve this because the clarification comes after the ambiguous phrase. While the authors likely mean 'our sequential algorithm' or 'the sequential two-sample test we propose', the phrasing is imprecise and could mislead a careful reader about the nature of the guarantees.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for empirical accuracy in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 5: Detect covariate shift if 0 _._ 5 _̸∈_ ΘCP(2 _wµw,t,_ 2 _w_ ; _α_ )
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the authors define the empirical accuracy as µ̂_w,t (with a hat), but in Algorithm 1 line 5 they write µw,t without a hat. The intended symbol is clearly µ̂_w,t, as the Clopper-Pearson interval requires the empirical estimate. This inconsistency could confuse readers about whether the true accuracy or its estimate is being used. It is a minor oversight in notation that should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Incorrect condition in false negative bound (Theorem 5)
comments.quote: If \(S = T_{w,t}\) and \(w - 1 - \lfloor ~~\sqrt{w \log(2/\alpha)} \rfloor \geq 0\)
comments.explanation: The theorem states 'If S = T_{w,t} ...'. However, the problem formulation defines the null hypothesis as S = \bar{T}_{w,t} (i.e., no shift). The false negative bound should apply under the alternative hypothesis when a shift exists (S ≠ \bar{T}_{w,t}). The earlier Lemma 2 and Theorem 3 correctly use S = \bar{T}_{w,t}. Here, the condition S = T_{w,t} (without the bar) is likely a typo; it should read 'S ≠ \bar{T}_{w,t}' or 'S = \bar{T}_{w,t}' if the bound is for the false positive rate, but the surrounding text assumes nontrivial accuracy (μ* ≥ 1/2+ε), which only holds under a shift. This is a serious inconsistency that would invalidate the theorem as stated. (Combined with related observation: throughout the problem formulation and other lemmas, the null hypothesis is consistently stated as S = T̄_w,t, where T̄_w,t is the average target distribution over the window. The notation Tw,t earlier denotes the sequence of target samples, not a distribution. Using Tw,t here is inconsistent and could mislead readers about which distributions are being compared.)

comments.title: Missing definition of Q_{w,t}
comments.quote: Let \(Q_{w,t}\) be a distribution over \(X \times \{ 0 , 1 \}\), where
comments.explanation: The text introduces a distribution Q_{w,t} but never defines it. The sentence ends with 'where' and no completion follows. This is a key component of the algorithm (it describes the mixture distribution for the window), and its absence leaves the reader guessing how the Bernoulli variable is constructed. The context later clarifies that the accuracy is estimated over the window, but the formal definition of Q_{w,t} is needed to understand the probability space.

comments.title: Missing definition of the empirical accuracy estimate
comments.quote: The unbiased empirical estimate of this accuracy is denoted by
comments.explanation: The text states that an unbiased empirical estimate of μ^*_{w,t} is used, but no formula is given. The estimator is presumably the average of the indicator variables over the window (e.g., (1/(2w)) Σ ...), but this is left implicit. While the phrase 'denoted by' suggests it will be given, the subsequent text jumps to 'In fact, 2w μ̂_{w,t} is a Binomial random variable...' without first defining μ̂_{w,t}. The reader must infer the definition, which could lead to ambiguity about the exact composition (e.g., whether both source and target predictions are counted).

comments.title: Missing completions for key probability statements
comments.quote: In fact, \(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}\) is a Binomial random variable with \(\text{Binomial}(2w, \mu^*_{w,t})\); thus, the accuracy \(\mu^*_{w,t}\) can be estimated by the Clopper-Pearson (CP) interval \(\Theta_{\text{CP}}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}, 2w; \alpha)\) that includes the unknown parameter \(\mu^*_{w,t}\) with high probability, \(i.e.\),
comments.explanation: The sentence cuts off after 'i.e.,' and does not provide the probability statement (e.g., P(μ* ∈ Θ_CP) ≥ 1−α). While a reader familiar with CP intervals may infer the coverage guarantee, the text explicitly promises a statement that is missing. This could mislead a careful reader trying to verify the correctness of the test.

comments.title: Grammatical error: 'want to w to be small'
comments.quote: this approach reduces sample efficiency, which is problematic in our setting since we often want to \(w\) to be small.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'want to w to be small' contains a stray 'to' after 'want'. It should read 'we often want w to be small.' This is a minor typographical/grammatical error that does not affect understanding but detracts from clarity.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence after 'where' in Bernoulli definition
comments.quote: Then, \(z = 1 (\hat{g}_t (x) = y)\) is a Bernoulli random variable with distribution \(\text{Bernoulli}(\mu^*_{w,t})\), where
comments.explanation: The sentence ends with 'where' and a line break, without completing the clause that should define μ^*_{w,t}. The next line begins with 'is the accuracy of \hat{g}...', which seems to be the continuation, but the punctuation and formatting suggest missing text (e.g., 'where μ^*_{w,t} = ...'). This could be an OCR artifact, but as presented, it is fragmentary and could confuse readers.

comments.title: Inconsistent condition for validity of false negative bound
comments.quote: the condition on _w_ suggests that the bound is valid when _w ≥_ 201 given _α_ = 0 _._ 01.
comments.explanation: The condition stated in Theorem 5 (and used here) is w − 1 − floor(sqrt(w log(2/α))) ≥ 0. For α = 0.01, log(2/0.01) = log(200) ≈ 5.2983, so sqrt(w·5.2983) is about 2.30·sqrt(w). Evaluating w = 100 gives sqrt(529.83) ≈ 23.02, floor = 23, 100 − 1 − 23 = 76 ≥ 0. Even w = 10 gives sqrt(52.98) ≈ 7.28, floor = 7, 10 − 1 − 7 = 2 ≥ 0. progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-level Feedback

This paper addresses a practically important problem—sequential covariate shift detection—and proposes a thoughtful adaptation of classifier two-sample tests to the online setting. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, particularly in the introduction and motivation, where the challenges of sequential data and multiple shift events are well-articulated. However, the paper suffers from a significant disconnect between its stated ambitions and its theoretical and empirical contributions. The authors claim to handle "multiple" shifts (e.g., shift, then shift back) but provide no theoretical guarantees for the false negative rate (FNR) under such non-monotonic shifts, and the FNR bounds presented in Section 4 are stated "under mild conditions" that are not verified or discussed in the context of realistic, complex data (e.g., ImageNet). Furthermore, the empirical evaluation, while benchmarked on ImageNet, appears to lack comparisons to baselines that are explicitly designed for sequential or online shift detection (e.g., sequential probability ratio tests, online MMD with sliding windows), making it unclear how the proposed method compares to existing alternatives in the sequential setting. The most critical issue is the paper's treatment of false positive control: the authors explicitly state they do not use a sequential test and only guarantee per-step FPR, yet this limitation is under-discussed relative to the practical need for low cumulative false alarms over long deployment horizons. Without a more rigorous treatment of sequential testing theory or an empirical study of cumulative FPR, the practical utility of the method remains questionable. The paper would also benefit from a clearer explanation of why online gradient steps preserve test validity—this is a key technical claim that is currently undersupported. Overall, while the core idea is promising and the writing is clear, the paper's contributions are not yet fully substantiated by its theory or experiments, and major revisions are needed to close the gap between the claimed generality and the provided evidence.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript addresses a pertinent and practically challenging problem by extending classifier two-sample tests to a sequential setting, offering a novel online algorithm that leverages the Clopper-Pearson interval to provide finite-sample false positive rate guarantees, thereby improving upon prior methods reliant on asymptotic approximations. The motivation is well-articulated, and the explicit distinction between this "sequential detection" framework and formal sequential hypothesis tests is a valuable clarification that correctly delineates the scope of error rate control. However, significant concerns remain regarding the computational tractability and statistical stability of training a neural discriminator online: the approach must address the non-stationarity of the discriminator's training distribution and memory constraints, as the online update strategy risks overfitting to recent test batches or failing to generalize if historical training data is not managed effectively. Furthermore, while the per-step false positive rate bounds are theoretically sound, the lack of uniform control over time implies that cumulative false alarm rates could accumulate significantly in long-running deployments, a practical limitation that warrants rigorous quantitative analysis rather than relying solely on anecdotal manageability. Finally, the false negative rate bounds depend on "mild conditions" that should be explicitly defined and empirically validated to ensure they do not impose overly restrictive assumptions on the classifier's capabilities in high-dimensional domains.

comments.title: Inconsistent and Inaccurate Description of Sequential Testing Framework
comments.quote: While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test... Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps. A sequential test only applies if _all_ of the test data is shifted.
comments.explanation: This consolidates three closely related methodological points: (1) Paragraph 6 contradicts Paragraph 9 by referencing FPR guarantees 'provided by the sequential test,' while Paragraph 9 explicitly states sequential tests are deliberately not used. (2) The claim that abandoning uniform FPR control is strictly 'necessary' to detect delayed shifts is an overstatement; adaptive change-point methods (e.g., CUSUM) can maintain uniform control while remaining sensitive to delayed shifts. (3) The justification that sequential tests only apply when *all* data is shifted is incorrect and misrepresents modern online testing frameworks (e.g., SPRT, e-values). The authors should clarify the distinction between the sequential setting and sequential testing, rephrase the necessity claim as a design trade-off, and correct the literature mischaracterization.

comments.title: Contradictory terminology regarding 'sequential test'
comments.quote: We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold.
comments.explanation: Initially, this phrase seemed out of place given the surrounding context. Upon checking the immediately following paragraph titled 'Sequential detection vs. sequential tests', the authors explicitly state: 'While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test... Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps.' The earlier mention of guarantees 'provided by the sequential test' directly contradicts this deliberate methodological choice. It is likely a leftover phrase from an earlier draft or a typo confusing the 'sequential setting' with 'sequential hypothesis testing' (e.g., SPRT). This creates genuine ambiguity about whether the method relies on classical sequential testing frameworks or their proposed online two-sample procedure, which could mislead readers attempting to reproduce or compare the approach.

comments.title: Overstatement on uniform FPR bounds and delayed shift detection
comments.quote: Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps. This is necessary: we cannot guarantee that we detect a covariate shift occurring at a later point in time if we constrain the false positive to be bounded uniformly across all steps.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that abandoning uniform FPR control is 'necessary' to detect delayed shifts. While it is true that uniform bounds (e.g., via Bonferroni correction or Brownian motion boundaries) tighten thresholds at early time steps and can significantly reduce statistical power for shifts that occur later, stating this as strictly 'necessary' is an overstatement. Adaptive change-point detection methods (e.g., CUSUM, Page's test) or weighted sequential tests can maintain uniform error control while remaining sensitive to delayed shifts. In the specific context of two-sample testing where early data comes from $H_0$ and later data from $H_1$, the standard i.i.d. assumptions of classical sequential tests are violated, which justifies their approach, but the phrasing implies a universal limitation rather than a design trade-off specific to their online two-sample formulation. Softening this to acknowledge the power-accuracy trade-off without claiming strict necessity would improve precision.

The classifier is a random object learned from data, not a fixed population parameter. The phrasing 'contains the true accuracy $\hat{g}_t$' is mathematically imprecise. While likely an OCR mangling of $\mu^*$ or a similar symbol, it should be corrected to explicitly reference the true accuracy parameter to maintain statistical rigor.

Modern sequential and online testing frameworks (e.g., SPRT, e-values, martingale-based tests) are specifically designed to detect shifts at an unknown stopping time without requiring the entire data stream to be shifted. While the authors' choice of per-step FPR control is valid and well-motivated for delayed/gradual shifts, this justification misrepresents the scope and capabilities of sequential testing methods, potentially misleading readers about the landscape of related work.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a practically relevant problem—detecting covariate shift in sequential data streams—while offering finite-sample theoretical guarantees that surpass previous asymptotic approximations. The clarity of the writing is high, effectively motivating the approach and distinguishing the proposed "online evaluation" method from batch-based C2ST. However, the most significant theoretical and practical issue is the relaxation of the false positive rate bound to be per-step rather than cumulative. In a continuous monitoring scenario (e.g., an autonomous vehicle), a non-uniform bound implies that the probability of a false alarm will eventually approach 1 over long horizons. The authors acknowledge this constraint in the introduction, but the paper must rigorously justify why this relaxation is acceptable and, crucially, define the stopping criteria or alert thresholds that account for this type I error accumulation. Additionally, while the core idea of evaluating the discriminator before an update is clever, the theoretical analysis must carefully account for the instability introduced by the model updating immediately after evaluating on the incoming point, ensuring that the "online evaluation" does not introduce bias in the empirical distribution of the test statistic.

comments.title: Incorrect Universal Claim on Baseline Performance

progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive_original__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a practically relevant problem—detecting covariate shift in sequential data streams—while offering finite-sample theoretical guarantees that surpass previous asymptotic approximations. The clarity of the writing is high, effectively motivating the approach and distinguishing the proposed "online evaluation" method from batch-based C2ST. However, the most significant theoretical and practical issue is the relaxation of the false positive rate bound to be per-step rather than cumulative. In a continuous monitoring scenario (e.g., an autonomous vehicle), a non-uniform bound implies that the probability of a false alarm will eventually approach 1 over long horizons. The authors acknowledge this constraint in the introduction, but the paper must rigorously justify why this relaxation is acceptable and, crucially, define the stopping criteria or alert thresholds that account for this type I error accumulation. Additionally, while the core idea of evaluating the discriminator before an update is clever, the theoretical analysis must carefully account for the instability introduced by the model updating immediately after evaluating on the incoming point, ensuring that the "online evaluation" does not introduce bias in the empirical distribution of the test statistic.
